numhelpr 0.0.0.9000

    • Introduction to numhelpr

Introduction to numhelpr

intro-to-numhelpr.Rmd
library(numhelpr)

Why numhelpr?

Working with numbers in R often requires writing the same small formulas over and over again. numhelpr solves three of the most common annoyances.

  • Formatting decimals as percentages
  • Calculating percent change between two values
  • Clamping values within a specified range

The Dataset

We will use the built-in sales_data dataset to demonstrate each function.

sales_data
#>       product price_old price_new rating raw_score
#> 1      Widget        10     12.50  0.873       1.8
#> 2      Gadget        25     22.00  0.654      -0.5
#> 3   Doohickey         5      5.75  0.921       3.2
#> 4 Thingamajig       100    115.00  0.102       0.7

percent()

Converting a decimal rating to a readable percentage normally requires multiplying by 100 and pasting a % sign. percent() does this in one step.

percent(sales_data$rating)
#> [1] "87.3%" "65.4%" "92.1%" "10.2%"

You can also control the number of decimal places:

percent(sales_data$rating, digits = 2)
#> [1] "87.3%" "65.4%" "92.1%" "10.2%"

percent_change()

Calculating percent change between old and new prices normally requires writing out the formula every time. percent_change() handles this cleanly.

percent_change(sales_data$price_old, sales_data$price_new)
#> [1]  25 -12  15  15

Positive values indicate a price increase, negative values a decrease.

clamp()

Sometimes raw scores need to be restricted to a valid range. clamp() caps values between a lower and upper bound.

clamp(sales_data$raw_score, lower = 0, upper = 2)
#> [1] 1.8 0.0 2.0 0.7

Any values below 0 is set to 0, and any value above 2 is set to 2.

On this page
